method0.generated_article:
==Geography==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was a volcanic island in the central Tongan archipelago, located in the South Pacific Ocean about 30 kilometres northwest of the Tongan capital, Nukuʻalofa, and within the Haʻapai island group. The island was composed of two older remnant islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, connected by a low volcanic land bridge formed during a period of activity from an underwater volcano. Before its destruction in 2022, the combined landform measured only a few kilometres across and rose steeply from the sea, with a shoreline shaped by cliffs, beaches of volcanic material, and a central low area.

The island lay within a maritime tropical environment and was frequently exposed to strong winds, surf, and storm systems. Its coastline was unstable because it consisted largely of unconsolidated ash, tuff, and volcanic rubble. The surrounding waters were relatively shallow in places, but the island itself stood on the summit region of a volcanic edifice rising from the seafloor.

==Geology==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was the emergent portion of the Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcanic complex, a submarine volcano in the Tonga-Kermadec volcanic arc. This arc is created by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate. The volcano is one of many along this geologically active boundary, where magma rises through the crust and periodic eruptions build volcanic islands.

Prior to the 2014–2015 eruption, the two remnant islands represented the eroded tops of older volcanic deposits. The eruption beginning in late 2014 produced a new tuff cone and connected the two older islands with fresh tephra and ash. This made Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai one of the few volcanic islands in recent history to persist long enough for scientists to study how newly formed land evolves under tropical marine conditions. Remote sensing and field observations showed rapid erosion of the new landform by waves and rainfall, but also the development of consolidated volcanic rock and the gradual establishment of soils in sheltered areas.

The island was ultimately destroyed or greatly reduced during the catastrophic 2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption, which involved massive explosive activity, caldera collapse, and widespread tsunami generation. The eruption removed much of the newly formed land and altered the surrounding seabed and volcanic structure.

==Ecology==
Despite its youth and unstable surface, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai became an important natural laboratory for ecological succession. Scientists observed that the island was initially nearly barren, but plant colonization began relatively quickly. Bird-borne dispersal, ocean drift, and wind transport were thought to have introduced seeds and microbial life. Over time, hardy coastal plants and grasses began to take root in limited areas, especially where ash and sand had stabilized.

The island also attracted seabirds, which nested or used it as a resting site. Their presence contributed to nutrient input through guano, accelerating soil development and helping support vegetation. The surrounding marine environment was inhabited by reef fish and other tropical ocean species, while the island’s shores provided nesting and haul-out space for marine life depending on sea conditions.

Because Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai existed for only a short period in its modern form, its ecosystem remained simple compared with older tropical islands. Nevertheless, it offered rare insight into how life colonizes a newly formed volcanic landmass in the open ocean.

==Climate==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai had a tropical maritime climate moderated by the surrounding ocean. Temperatures were warm year-round, with relatively small seasonal variation. Rainfall was influenced by the South Pacific convergence zone, trade winds, and tropical storm activity. The island was exposed to high humidity, heavy seasonal rain at times, and occasional cyclones, all of which contributed to erosion and the breakdown of loose volcanic deposits.

The marine setting also meant that salt spray and wave action strongly affected vegetation and surface stability. On such a small island, microclimates varied significantly between exposed coasts and more sheltered interior areas, influencing where plants could establish and survive.

==History==
The island’s modern history is closely tied to volcanic activity. Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai were long known as separate islands in Tonga. During the 19th and 20th centuries, they were noted by navigators and mapped as small, rugged volcanic remnants. The gap between them was later filled by new volcanic material during the 2014–2015 eruption of the Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai submarine volcano, creating a single larger island.

The 2014–2015 eruption was important scientifically because it produced a landform that persisted far longer than expected. Initial explosive phases generated steam and ash, followed by the construction of a tuff cone and widespread deposition of volcanic material. Researchers from several countries studied the island as a rare example of a newborn volcanic island that could be examined in near real time. Observations of erosion, vegetation colonization, and shoreline change were used to improve understanding of island formation and landscape evolution.

In January 2022, a much larger eruption occurred at the same volcanic system. This event was among the most powerful volcanic eruptions in recent decades and was accompanied by a destructive tsunami across parts of the Pacific. The eruption largely obliterated the island, leaving only small remnants of the former landmass above sea level, if any persisted. The event also generated global interest because of the scale of atmospheric shock waves, submarine explosion, and environmental impact.

==Scientific significance==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai became one of the most studied small volcanic islands in the world because it provided a rare opportunity to observe the early stages of island development. It was compared with other young volcanic landforms, including Surtsey in Iceland, though the tropical marine setting of Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai made its ecological and geomorphic evolution distinct.

Scientists used the island to study volcanic landbuilding, coastal erosion, sediment consolidation, microbial colonization, and the arrival of plants and animals on isolated land. Satellite imagery, aerial surveys, and field expeditions documented how quickly the island changed under tropical wave attack. After the 2022 eruption, research interest expanded to include the role of explosive submarine volcanism in shaping islands, oceans, and the atmosphere.

The island’s brief existence in its modern form provided a valuable case study in the interaction of volcanism, geomorphology, ecology, and climate in a remote oceanic setting.

method1.generated_article:
==Formation and geography==

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a volcanic island in the South Pacific formed during an eruption of an underwater volcano near Tonga in late 2014 and early 2015, when steam, ash, and rock built up a new landmass between two older islands.[1] The island had a summit of about 400 feet and was visible to satellites after the eruption had finished depositing ash in January 2015.[1] The landform was named for its neighboring islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[1]

==Scientific significance==

Scientists studied the island because its formation offered a rare opportunity to observe the birth and early evolution of new land in real time.[1] Observations, satellite imagery, samples, and three-dimensional topographical maps suggested that warm seawater chemically altered ash into a more durable material, helping the island persist longer than expected.[1] Researchers also noted that the island could provide clues about how land and possibly life may have developed on early Mars.[1]

==2022 eruption==

On 15 January 2022, Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai erupted violently, producing the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments.[2][4] The eruption generated a tsunami that affected Tonga and many other parts of the Pacific, including Fiji, American Samoa, Vanuatu, New Zealand, Japan, the United States, the Russian Far East, Chile, and Peru.[1] The eruption also caused a sonic boom heard far from the volcano and was described by scientists as a major natural explosion in the geophysical record.[5]

==Atmospheric and climate effects==

The 2022 eruption injected vast quantities of water vapor into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water by about 10%, with much of that excess still present through 2025.[2][4] It also released sulfur dioxide that was converted into sulfate aerosols, which reflect sunlight and can cool the atmosphere.[3][5] Studies found that the eruption did not warm the planet; instead, it produced a slight cooling effect in the Southern Hemisphere of about 0.1 °C, while any northern warming influence was small.[3] Broader assessments concluded that the eruption’s effects on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate were minor, and that it did not cause the record-high global temperatures of 2023–2024.[2][4]

==Research and monitoring==

The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption became the focus of a major international scientific collaboration, including the Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai Impact Model Observation Comparison project.[2] A seven-chapter assessment report brought together 159 scientists from 21 countries to study the eruption’s cloud evolution, atmospheric chemistry, dynamics, ozone impacts, and radiative effects.[2] Later modeling studies also emphasized that the eruption’s shockwaves and energy release were extraordinary, with one analysis describing the event as causing the Earth to “ring like a bell.”[5]

sections.content: Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a volcanic island in the South Pacific formed during an eruption of an underwater volcano near Tonga in late 2014 and early 2015, when steam, ash, and rock built up a new landmass between two older islands.[1] The island had a summit of about 400 feet and was visible to satellites after the eruption had finished depositing ash in January 2015.[1] The landform was named for its neighboring islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[1]

method2.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was an active submarine volcano whose edifice included the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, the visible remnants of a larger volcanic structure around a central caldera.[4] The islands had previously been joined by a young volcanic cone, and sonar surveys before the 2022 eruption revealed a large 4 × 2 km depression consistent with caldera collapse.[4] Earlier eruptions in 2014–2015 built up the islands, but by late 2021 the volcano still appeared broadly similar to its post-2015 state.[4]  

On 15 January 2022, the volcano erupted violently and produced a plume that rose more than 30 km into the atmosphere, with overshooting tops above 55 km.[5] The eruption generated one of the largest underwater explosions ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and was estimated to have released energy on the order of 10–28 exajoules.[1][5] It may have been a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and has been discussed as possibly reaching volcanic explosivity index 5, though further analysis is needed to confirm the total erupted volume.[2]  

The eruption destroyed most of the small islands, leaving only minor fragments at the eastern and western edges of the former landmass.[2] Subsequent surveys found that the caldera floor had deepened substantially, from about 500 feet below sea level in 2015 to about 2,800 feet below sea level in May 2022, indicating major collapse and removal of material from the summit region.[3] Scientists also detected continuing minor eruptions and signs of ongoing volcanic activity in the caldera in later surveys.[3]

==Geography==
The island was located in the Kingdom of Tonga in the South Pacific, where an underwater volcano erupted in December 2014 and built a new landmass between the older islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[1][2] Tephra from the eruption accumulated around a new volcanic cone, joining the two islands into a single conjoined island.[2] The island’s formation was notable because it emerged in the satellite era and was observed directly by satellites and other scientific instruments.[1][2]  

At its peak, the island had a summit about 400 feet high, though later surveys projected that it could have a limited lifespan because wave erosion and other marine processes would gradually wear it down.[1] By 2022, most of the island had already been destroyed or submerged following a major eruption on 15 January that year.[1][2] After that eruption, a majority of the island was underwater, leaving only reduced remnants above sea level.[1]  

The island sat close enough to the erupting volcano to be engulfed by the 2022 event, which generated shockwaves and pressure waves that were felt across the Pacific and a tsunami that affected many countries around the basin.[2][4][5] Its position in the Tonga archipelago made it part of a geographically unusual volcanic setting, and its rapid creation and destruction drew scientific interest for what it could reveal about volcanic island formation and erosion.[1][3]

==Eruptions==
The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai volcanic system erupted violently on 15 January 2022, with the main explosion beginning in the afternoon local time and sending gas and ash tens of miles into the atmosphere.[4] The event was the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments and was described as one of the most explosive eruptions of the satellite era.[2][3] The eruption generated a massive tsunami that struck Tonga and other Pacific coasts, destroying buildings, displacing residents, and causing fatalities in Tonga and Peru.[4] It also produced a sonic boom that was heard around the world twice.[3]  

The eruption injected an extraordinary amount of water vapor into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water by about 10%.[2][5] It also released sulfur dioxide, which formed sulfate aerosols that reflected sunlight back into space.[1][5] Because volcanic aerosols and water vapor have opposing climate effects, scientists initially expected the eruption to cause notable warming, but later studies found that the net effect was instead a slight cooling, including a reduction of temperatures over the Southern Hemisphere by about 0.1 °C.[1][5]  

Scientists also found that the eruption perturbed stratospheric ozone in the Southern Hemisphere, although the overall effect on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate was minor.[2] The water vapor injected by the eruption has persisted in the atmosphere for years, with much of it remaining through 2025.[2] Research on the eruption has been aided by satellite observations, balloon campaigns, and ground-based monitoring networks that tracked the plume, aerosols, water vapor, and trace gases from minutes after the eruption onward.[2][5]

==Footnotes==
The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption on 15 January 2022 was one of the most explosive volcanic events of the modern era, producing a vertical plume that rose more than 50 km above Earth.[2] It generated atmospheric waves that propagated around the world multiple times, including Lamb waves detected by satellite and ground-based observations.[2] The eruption’s initial explosion released an estimated 10 to 28 exajoules of energy, possibly exceeding the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption and perhaps approaching the scale of Krakatoa in 1883.[2] It also sent shockwaves through the ground, water, and air, with later analyses concluding that the event released an order of magnitude more energy than the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens.[1] Scientists described the event as effectively making the Earth “ring like a bell.”[1] The eruption also produced an umbrella cloud, local and distant tsunamis, and widespread atmospheric effects, including major changes in water vapor and sulfur dioxide distributions.[3][5]

sections.content:
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was an active submarine volcano whose edifice included the islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, the visible remnants of a larger volcanic structure around a central caldera.[4] The islands had previously been joined by a young volcanic cone, and sonar surveys before the 2022 eruption revealed a large 4 × 2 km depression consistent with caldera collapse.[4] Earlier eruptions in 2014–2015 built up the islands, but by late 2021 the volcano still appeared broadly similar to its post-2015 state.[4]

method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a submarine volcano in the Tonga island arc whose emergent islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, were the visible remnants of the rim of a larger caldera.[4] High-resolution sonar surveys had already identified a roughly 4 × 2 km depression about 150 m deep at the volcano’s center, consistent with an explosive caldera formed by collapse of an earlier volcanic structure.[4] The eruption that began on 15 January 2022 was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may have reached volcanic explosivity index 5, although further analysis was needed to confirm the total volume of erupted material.[2] During the eruption, the two islands that had been joined by a new volcanic cone were largely blown apart, leaving only small fragments at the western and eastern edges.[2] Subsequent seafloor surveys found that the caldera floor had deepened markedly by May 2022, from about 500 feet below sea level in 2015 to about 2,800 feet below sea level, indicating major collapse of the volcanic edifice.[3] Scientists estimated that about 1.5 cubic miles of material had disappeared from the caldera since the 2015 survey.[3] The eruption also produced massive explosions, sent gas and ash more than 36 miles into the atmosphere, and generated tsunamis that damaged communities across Tonga and much of the Pacific basin.[3] More recent research suggested that the event produced the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and injected an unprecedented amount of water vapor into the stratosphere.[1]

==Geography==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was a conjoined volcanic island in the Kingdom of Tonga, formed in late 2014 when tephra from an undersea eruption built up around a new cone and joined the pre-existing islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[2] The island was a short-lived landmass in the South Pacific and was closely monitored by satellite, which documented its rapid emergence and later changes in shape and extent.[1][2]

By early 2015, the new island had a summit rising about 400 feet (120 m) above sea level and sat between the two older islands that gave it its name.[1] Scientists noted that warm seawater interacting with volcanic ash may have chemically altered the loose deposits into more durable rock, helping explain why the island persisted for several years despite initial expectations of a much shorter lifespan.[1]

The island’s geography changed dramatically after the 15 January 2022 eruption, when much of the landmass was destroyed and a majority of it became submerged.[1] Observations after the eruption showed that the island’s remaining surface was heavily reduced, leaving the feature largely underwater.[1][2]

The 2022 eruption also generated powerful atmospheric and oceanic effects across the region, including tsunami waves that affected islands throughout Tonga and shock waves that were detected far beyond the South Pacific.[2][4][5] These events made Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai a major focus of post-disaster field surveys and scientific study.[3][4]

==Eruptions==
The volcano’s 15 January 2022 eruption was exceptionally explosive and sent material high into the atmosphere, with reports describing ash and gas reaching tens of kilometers above the sea surface.[3][4] It was the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and produced the most explosive event of the satellite era.[2]  

The eruption injected an unusually large amount of water vapor into the stratosphere, raising global stratospheric water content by about 10 percent.[2][5] Scientists initially worried that this added moisture could amplify warming, but later studies found that sulfate aerosols and other particles also played a major role in reflecting sunlight back into space.[1][5]  

As a result, the eruption did not produce the warming once feared; instead, it had a small net cooling effect on the Southern Hemisphere, with one study estimating a reduction of about 0.1 °C.[1] Researchers also found that the overall impacts on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate were minor, despite measurable short-term stratospheric ozone perturbations in the Southern Hemisphere.[2]  

The event became an important case study for atmospheric science because it demonstrated how water-rich submarine eruptions can alter the stratosphere in complex ways.[1][2] It was also closely tracked by satellites, balloon campaigns, and ground-based observing networks, which allowed scientists to follow the plume, aerosols, water vapor, and trace gases from the eruption onward.[2][5]

==Footnotes==
The January 2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption was widely regarded as one of the most explosive volcanic events of the modern era, producing a plume that rose more than 50 km above the Earth’s surface.[2][5]  
Scientists described it as the largest natural explosion ever recorded in the geophysical record, with the eruption generating shockwaves that propagated through the ground, water, and atmosphere.[1][2]  
The event sent atmospheric waves around the world multiple times, including Lamb waves traveling at roughly 318 m/s near the surface.[2]  
It also produced a vertical plume reaching the mesosphere, with some studies reporting altitudes of up to 57–58 km.[3][5]  
The eruption triggered global tsunami alerts, local and distant tsunamis, and ionospheric disturbances, and it had significant effects on Tonga and surrounding countries.[3][5]  
Research on the eruption also found unusual atmospheric impacts, including enhanced lightning activity and a substantial increase in stratospheric water vapor.[1]  
One study estimated that the initial explosion released between 10 and 28 exajoules of energy, possibly exceeding the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and approaching the scale of Krakatoa in 1883.[2]  
The eruption further caused major damage in Tonga, with only small parts of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai remaining after the event.[3]

sections.content: Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a submarine volcano in the Tonga island arc whose emergent islands, Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, were the visible remnants of the rim of a larger caldera.[4] High-resolution sonar surveys had already identified a roughly 4 × 2 km depression about 150 m deep at the volcano’s center, consistent with an explosive caldera formed by collapse of an earlier volcanic structure.[4] The eruption that began on 15 January 2022 was described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may have reached volcanic explosivity index 5, although further analysis was needed to confirm the total volume of erupted material.[2] During the eruption, the two islands that had been joined by a new volcanic cone were largely blown apart, leaving only small fragments at the western and eastern edges.[2] Subsequent seafloor surveys found that the caldera floor had deepened markedly by May 2022, from about 500 feet below sea level in 2015 to about 2,800 feet below sea level, indicating major collapse of the volcanic edifice.[3] Scientists estimated that about 1.5 cubic miles of material had disappeared from the caldera since the 2015 survey.[3] The eruption also produced massive explosions, sent gas and ash more than 36 miles into the atmosphere, and generated tsunamis that damaged communities across Tonga and much of the Pacific basin.[3] More recent research suggested that the event produced the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and injected an unprecedented amount of water vapor into the stratosphere.[1]

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai was a conjoined volcanic island in the Kingdom of Tonga, formed in late 2014 when tephra from an undersea eruption built up around a new cone and joined the pre-existing islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[2] The island was a short-lived landmass in the South Pacific and was closely monitored by satellite, which documented its rapid emergence and later changes in shape and extent.[1][2]

By early 2015, the new island had a summit rising about 400 feet (120 m) above sea level and sat between the two older islands that gave it its name.[1] Scientists noted that warm seawater interacting with volcanic ash may have chemically altered the loose deposits into more durable rock, helping explain why the island persisted for several years despite initial expectations of a much shorter lifespan.[1]

The island’s geography changed dramatically after the 15 January 2022 eruption, when much of the landmass was destroyed and a majority of it became submerged.[1] Observations after the eruption showed that the island’s remaining surface was heavily reduced, leaving the feature largely underwater.[1][2]

The 2022 eruption also generated powerful atmospheric and oceanic effects across the region, including tsunami waves that affected islands throughout Tonga and shock waves that were detected far beyond the South Pacific.[2][4][5] These events made Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai a major focus of post-disaster field surveys and scientific study.[3][4]

The volcano’s 15 January 2022 eruption was exceptionally explosive and sent material high into the atmosphere, with reports describing ash and gas reaching tens of kilometers above the sea surface.[3][4] It was the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and produced the most explosive event of the satellite era.[2]  

The eruption injected an unusually large amount of water vapor into the stratosphere, raising global stratospheric water content by about 10 percent.[2][5] Scientists initially worried that this added moisture could amplify warming, but later studies found that sulfate aerosols and other particles also played a major role in reflecting sunlight back into space.[1][5]  

As a result, the eruption did not produce the warming once feared; instead, it had a small net cooling effect on the Southern Hemisphere, with one study estimating a reduction of about 0.1 °C.[1] Researchers also found that the overall impacts on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate were minor, despite measurable short-term stratospheric ozone perturbations in the Southern Hemisphere.[2]  

The event became an important case study for atmospheric science because it demonstrated how water-rich submarine eruptions can alter the stratosphere in complex ways.[1][2] It was also closely tracked by satellites, balloon campaigns, and ground-based observing networks, which allowed scientists to follow the plume, aerosols, water vapor, and trace gases from the eruption onward.[2][5]

The January 2022 Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai eruption was widely regarded as one of the most explosive volcanic events of the modern era, producing a plume that rose more than 50 km above the Earth’s surface.[2][5]  
Scientists described it as the largest natural explosion ever recorded in the geophysical record, with the eruption generating shockwaves that propagated through the ground, water, and atmosphere.[1][2]  
The event sent atmospheric waves around the world multiple times, including Lamb waves traveling at roughly 318 m/s near the surface.[2]  
It also produced a vertical plume reaching the mesosphere, with some studies reporting altitudes of up to 57–58 km.[3][5]  
The eruption triggered global tsunami alerts, local and distant tsunamis, and ionospheric disturbances, and it had significant effects on Tonga and surrounding countries.[3][5]  
Research on the eruption also found unusual atmospheric impacts, including enhanced lightning activity and a substantial increase in stratospheric water vapor.[1]  
One study estimated that the initial explosion released between 10 and 28 exajoules of energy, possibly exceeding the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and approaching the scale of Krakatoa in 1883.[2]  
The eruption further caused major damage in Tonga, with only small parts of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai remaining after the event.[3]
